ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fish ball noodle soup; closest_parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for fish ball noodle; A0FHR (Noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for fish ball noodle as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: rice noodles (bee hoon or kway teow or yellow noodles)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of fish ball noodle as served in a Singapore hawker centre or casual restaurant, approximately 450 g, comprising noodles, fish balls, broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Ball Noodle Soup — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo reports nutrient values for fish ball noodle soup (dry and soup variants). Per-100 g values are derived from the HPB Foodo entry for fish ball noodle (soup style), which is the closest match to this Singaporean hawker preparation. The restaurant version (Hai Kah Lang 'Ping Pong' oversized fish balls) may have slightly higher protein due to larger fish ball mass; values are adapted from the HPB standard reference and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for fish balls and cooked rice noodles.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles and mixed noodle dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean fish ball noodle. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles / rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low category) and mixed Asian noodle soup dishes (GI range 40–58). Fish balls and broth contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 46.8) / 100 ≈ 24.3, rounded to 24.4. Serving carbohydrate = 10.4 g per 100 g × 450 g = 46.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A standard 450 g bowl provides approximately 1710 mg sodium, which represents 74–114% of the recommended daily sodium limit (1500–2300 mg) for adults with hypertension; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit the soup liquid, and monitor overall daily sodium intake carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 24 per bowl, fish ball noodle has a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider portion control, choose thinner rice noodles or reduce noodle quantity, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from fish balls and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1710 mg per bowl) poses a significant cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; saturated fat is moderate at approximately 2.25 g per bowl, and the lean fish protein is cardioprotective, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit soup consumption, choose dry noodle variants, and request less seasoning to reduce overall sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Ball Noodle (鱼丸粉, Yú Wán Fěn) is a beloved Singaporean hawker dish consisting of springy fish balls served over or in a bowl of noodles — most commonly rice vermicelli (bee hoon), flat rice noodles (kway teow), or yellow wheat noodles — in a clear, savoury fish-based broth. The dish is a staple of Chinese-Singaporean cuisine with Teochew culinary roots, widely available at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and casual restaurants across Singapore. Fish balls are made from minced white fish (typically wolf herring or yellowtail) blended with starch, salt, and seasoning, then hand-rolled or machine-formed into smooth, bouncy spheres. The 'ping pong' or jumbo variant, as served at Hai Kah Lang, features oversized fish balls with a characteristically firm yet yielding texture and a clean, mild fish flavour. The broth is typically prepared by simmering fish bones and heads for several hours, yielding a light, umami-rich stock seasoned with soy sauce, white pepper, and sesame oil. Garnishes include sliced fish cake, bean sprouts, spring onions, and fried shallots. Chilli sauce and sambal are commonly served on the side. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~78 kcal per 100 g), provides lean fish protein (~5.2 g per 100 g), and is relatively low in fat (~1.8 g per 100 g). The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g, or ~1710 mg per standard 450 g bowl), which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low), making it a moderate glycemic load meal suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is managed. The dish is halal-certified at most establishments and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fish ball noodle soup reference, which is the closest approved primary source for this Singaporean hawker dish. The specific restaurant variant (Hai Kah Lang oversized ping pong fish balls) may differ slightly in protein and energy density due to larger fish ball mass. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle entries. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact HPB Foodo entry for this specific restaurant preparation was available; values are adapted from the standard HPB fish ball noodle reference.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish category. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish category. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish category. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) — fish ball noodle contains no industrially produced trans fats; this is consistent with HPB and USDA data for similar preparations. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (52, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle entries; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean fish ball noodle was found in the approved GI literature. 6. Sodium content is notably high (~380 mg/100 g; ~1710 mg per 450 g bowl), primarily from fish broth and seasoning; this is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. 7. The restaurant source (Hai Kah Lang, Funan Mall) serves a signature oversized 'ping pong' fish ball variant; nutrient values are adapted from the HPB standard fish ball noodle reference and may underestimate protein slightly for this specific preparation. 8. Food ID GD001776 originates from GrabFood Singapore seed data; this record has been fully processed under Veritas v2.
